critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks comparison to recent multi-turn dialogue program synthesis methods, which weakens the novelty claim of multi-turn prompting as a new paradigm.
critique_points.point_002: There is no ablation study analyzing the contribution of each sequential training dataset (THEPILE, BIGQUERY, BIGPYTHON) to model performance improvements.
critique_points.point_003: The effect of instruction turn ordering, length, and granularity on model performance in the multi-turn benchmark is not systematically investigated.
critique_points.point_004: The fairness of the comparison between multi-turn and single-turn concatenated prompts is unclear, as equivalence in content or length is not explicitly confirmed.
critique_points.point_005: The evaluation of multi-turn prompting only includes CODEGEN variants and omits competitive multi-turn capable baselines like Codex, limiting generalizability of findings.
critique_points.point_006: There is no evaluation of fine-tuned or prompt-tuned models on multi-turn data to assess whether multi-turn prompting truly provides advantages over such baselines.
critique_points.point_007: Details on tokenizer choice, code indentation, line breaks, and special tokens used in multi-turn inputs are missing, hindering reproducibility of perplexity and pass@k results.
critique_points.point_008: Although JAXFORMER is open sourced, the paper does not clarify availability of training scripts, hyperparameter settings, or reproducibility checklists needed to replicate CODEGEN training exactly.

summary: This paper presents CODEGEN, an open-source family of large autoregressive language models trained on mixed natural language and code datasets up to 16.1B parameters, achieving competitive code generation performance comparable to proprietary models like Codex. It proposes a multi-turn program synthesis paradigm, decomposing complex programming tasks into sequential natural language turns, and introduces a Multi-Turn Programming Benchmark (MTPB) to evaluate this approach. Empirical results demonstrate that multi-turn prompting reduces prompt perplexity and improves functional correctness, particularly on challenging tasks, with benefits emerging at larger model scales. The work also releases a TPU-optimized training library, JAXFORMER, and models to foster further research in program synthesis with large language models.
strengths: Introduction of a large-scale open-source code generation model family competitive with state-of-the-art proprietary models.
Novel multi-turn program synthesis paradigm supported by a new benchmark (MTPB) and empirical evidence showing improved understanding and code correctness.
Comprehensive evaluation across model scales and datasets, showing scaling laws and qualitative insights.
Release of an efficient TPU-based training library and model checkpoints to enable reproducibility and future research.
weaknesses: Lack of comparison to recent interactive or multi-turn program synthesis methods limits the novelty claim.
Absence of ablation studies to isolate effects of training data stages and instruction turn design choices.
Limited baseline diversity and missing fine-tuning or prompt-tuning baselines reduce confidence in generalizability of multi-turn prompting benefits.
Insufficient reproducibility details regarding tokenizer, input formatting, and training configurations.
No discussion on handling ambiguous or contradictory instructions or the practicality of multi-turn prompting in real user scenarios.
questions: How does the multi-turn prompting approach compare to recent dialogue-based or interactive program synthesis methods in terms of performance and usability?
What is the impact of instruction turn order, length, and granularity on model accuracy and efficiency in multi-turn synthesis?
Can fine-tuning or prompt-tuning on multi-turn data close the gap between single-turn and multi-turn prompting efficacy?
How robust is the multi-turn approach when instructions are ambiguous, contradictory, or user-generated rather than expert-crafted?
Are there plans to extend evaluation beyond Python to other programming languages or domain-specific languages to assess generalizability?
title: CodeGen: An Open Large Language Model for Code with Multi-Turn Program Synthesis
